The call to Noah is very kind, like a tender father calling his children indoors when he sees night or a storm coming. Noah did not go into the ark until God told him, though he knew it would be his place of refuge. It is comforting to see God going before us in every step we take. Noah had worked hard to build the ark, and now he was kept alive inside it. What we do in obedience to God’s command and in faith, we ourselves will certainly experience the comfort of, eventually. This call to Noah reminds us of the call that the gospel gives to poor sinners. Christ is an ark, in whom alone we can be safe when death and judgment approach. The word says, “Come;” ministers say, “Come;” the Spirit says, “Come, come into the Ark.” Noah was accounted righteous, not for his own righteousness, but as an heir of the righteousness which comes by faith. He believed in a savior and sought and expected salvation through Him alone. Thus he was justified by faith and received that Spirit whose fruit is goodness; but whoever does not have the Spirit of Christ is not His. After one hundred twenty years, God granted seven more days for repentance. But those seven days were wasted, like all the rest. They had only one week left, one Sabbath left to improve and consider things related to their peace. But it is common for those who have been careless about their souls during healthy years, when they viewed death as distant, to be equally careless during the few days of sickness when death approaches; their hearts being hardened by the deceitfulness of sin. As Noah prepared the ark by faith in the warning that a flood would come, so he entered it by faith in this warning that it would come quickly. And on the day Noah was securely fixed in the ark, the fountains of the great deep were broken up. The earth contained those waters, which at God’s command sprang up and flooded it; and similarly our bodies contain humors that, when God pleases, become the seeds and beginnings of mortal diseases. The windows of heaven were opened, and the waters above the firmament, that is, in the air, were poured out on the earth. Rain fell in drops; but such rains came as had never been known before or since. It rained without stopping, forty days and forty nights, over the entire earth at once. Because there was a special display of God’s almighty power in causing the flood, it is pointless and presumptuous to try explaining its method with human wisdom.

The wild creatures were made gentle and manageable; yet when this occasion was over, they were the same as before, because the ark did not change their natures. Hypocrites in the church, who outwardly conform to the laws of that ark, are still unchanged; and eventually it will be revealed what kind of people they are. God continued his care of Noah. God shut the door to secure him and keep him safe in the ark, also to keep everyone else out forever. How this was done is something God has not chosen to reveal. There is much of our gospel duty and privilege to be seen in Noah’s safety in the ark. The apostle uses it as a type of Christian baptism. Observe then, it is our great duty, in obedience to the gospel call, by lively faith in Christ, to come into that way of salvation which God has provided for poor sinners. Those who come into the ark should bring as many as they can with them, through good instruction, persuasion, and example. There is room enough in Christ for all comers. God put Adam into paradise but did not shut him in, so he threw himself out; but when God put Noah into the ark, and similarly when He brings a soul to Christ, salvation is sure: it is not in our own keeping, but in the Mediator’s hand. But the door of mercy will soon be shut against those who now make light of it. Knock now, and it will be opened.

The flood was increasing for forty days. The waters rose so high that the tops of the highest mountains were overflowed more than twenty feet. There is no place on earth so high as to set men out of the reach of God’s judgments. God's hand will find all his enemies. When the flood increased, Noah’s ark was lifted up, and the waters which destroyed everything else bore up the ark. What betokens death to those who do not believe, betokens life to life for the faithful.

All the men, women, and children in the world died, except those in the ark. We can easily imagine what terror seized them. Our Savior tells us that until the day the flood came, they were eating and drinking; they were deaf and blind to all divine warnings. They were caught off guard by death. They realized their folly when it was too late. We may suppose they tried every possible way to save themselves, but all in vain. And those who are not found in Christ, the Ark, are certainly undone, undone forever. Let us pause and consider this tremendous judgment! Who can stand before the Lord when he is angry? The sin of sinners will be their ruin, eventually, if it is not repented of. The righteous God knows how to bring ruin upon the world of the ungodly. How tremendous will be the day of judgment and destruction for ungodly men! Happy are those who are part of Christ’s family and safe with him as such; they may look forward without fear, and rejoice that they will triumph when fire burns up the earth and all that is in it. We tend to think there is something special about our own case or character; but if we neglect, refuse, or abuse the salvation of Christ, we will be destroyed along with the common ruin of an unbelieving world.
